Zora Neale Hurston in the 1920s in which the protagonist prays for hardship to be visited upon her arch enemies, not unlike the prophet Jonah.<|17.60|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.24|> The curse concludes saying,<|3.62|> <|5.18|> I ask for all these things<|8.22|> <|8.22|> because they have dragged me in the dust<|11.02|> <|11.02|> destruction.<|12.16|> <|13.70|> But then, in only a way Walker can,<|18.24|> <|18.24|> she makes a surprising detour in the final few paragraphs.<|22.42|> <|22.42|> Quote, "However,<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Just as the sun shines on the godly and ungodly alike, so does nuclear radiation.<|6.58|> <|8.40|> And with this knowledge, it becomes increasingly difficult to embrace the thought of extinction purely for the assumed satisfaction of, from the grave, achieving revenge."<|23.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Revenge may or may not be satisfying, she says.<|5.88|> <|6.66|> Who knows?<|7.40|> <|8.92|> Life, she then goes on to say, is better than death,<|13.62|> <|14.62|> if only because it is less boring and because it has end in suspended animation.<|9.92|> <|11.54|> Instead of tidy resolution, we are left with an unsettling question.<|16.78|> <|17.78|> What if my desire for another's destruction is actually tied to my own destruction?<|25.34|> <|26.50|> What if my desire for another's destruction is actually tied to my own destruction?<|27.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And what if the experience of eating a peach, getting lost in its sweetness and texture, is the thing standing between life today and destruction tomorrow?<|16.00|><|endoftext|>
